metadata.name: minecraft-resource-pack
metadata.summary: A Python library reading Minecraft's various resource pack formats.

class_variables.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.Transparency.FullOpaque

class_variables.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.Transparency.Partial

classes.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.__init__
documentation:
---
param face_width: the number of vertices per face (3 or 4)
param faces: |-
  a dictionary of numpy int arrays (stored under cull direction) containing
  the vertex indexes (\<face_width> columns) and
  texture index (1 column)
param texture_coords: a numpy float array containing the texture coordinate data.
  One line per vertex
param texture_index: true
param textures: true
param tint_verts: a numpy bool array if the vertex should have a tint applied to it.
  One line per vertex
param transparency: is the model a full non-transparent block
param verts: a numpy float array containing the vert data. One line per vertex
---

Workflow:

: find the directions a block is not being culled in
  look them up in the face table
  the face table will tell you which vertices are needed for the face

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.face_mode
documentation: The number of vertices per face

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.vert_tables
documentation: A dictionary of cull dir -> the flat vert table containing vertices, texture coords and (in the future) normals

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.verts
documentation:
A dictionary mapping face cull direction to the vertex table for that direction.
The vertex table is a flat numpy array who's length is a multiple of 3.
x,y,z coordinates.

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.texture_coords
documentation:
A dictionary mapping face cull direction to the texture coords table for that direction.
The texture coords table is a flat numpy array who's length is a multiple of 2.
tx, ty

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.tint_verts
documentation:
A dictionary mapping face cull direction to the tint table for that direction.
The tint table is a flat numpy bool array with three values per vertex.

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.faces
documentation:
A dictionary mapping face cull direction to the face table for that direction.
The face table is a flat numpy array of multiple 3 or 4 depending on face_mode.
First 3 or 4 columns index into the verts table.
Last column indexes into textures.

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.texture_index
documentation:
A dictionary mapping face cull direction to the face table for that direction.
The face table is a flat numpy array of multiple 2 indexing into textures.

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.textures
documentation: A list of all the texture paths.

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.is_opaque
documentation:
If the model covers all surrounding blocks.
Also takes into account texture transparency.

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.is_transparent
documentation:
The transparency mode of the block
0 - the block is a full block with opaque textures
1 - the block is a full block with transparent/translucent textures
2 - the block is not a full block

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.mesh.block.block_mesh.BlockMesh.rotate
documentation:
Create a rotated version of this block model. Culling directions are also rotated.
rotx and roty must be ints in the range -3 to 3 inclusive.
Class for storing model data

A class to hold the bare-bones information about the resource pack.

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.amulet.block.Block.blockstate
documentation:
The Java blockstate string of this Block object (Eg: `minecraft:stone`, `minecraft:oak_log[axis=x]`)
Note if there are extra blocks this will only show the base block.
Note this will only contain properties with TAG_String values.
\:return: The blockstate string

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.amulet.block.Block.snbt_blockstate
documentation:
The SNBT blockstate string of this Block object (Eg: `minecraft:bell[attachment="standing",direction=0,toggle_bit=0b]`)
Note if there are extra blocks this will only show the base block.
\:return: The blockstate string

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.amulet.block.Block.full_blockstate
documentation:
The SNBT blockstate string of the base block and extra blocks (Eg: `minecraft:fence[wood_type="oak"]{minecraft:water[liquid_depth=0]}`).
\:return: The blockstate string

return

\:return: A tuple of Block objects

The Block object to add to the end of this Block object's

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.amulet.block.Block.__sub__

methods.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.amulet.block.Block.remove_layer
documentation:
Removes the Block object from the specified layer and returns the resulting new Block object

```
param layer

The layer of extra block to remove

return

A new instance of Block with the same data but with the extra block at specified layer removed

raises 

Raised when you remove the base block from a Block with no other extra blocks
```

class_variables.name: minecraft_model_reader.api.amulet.block.Block.__slots__
documentation: Reduces memory footprint

Class to handle data about various blockstates and allow for extra blocks to be created and interacted with.

:::{important}
Creating version specific block objects via the `Block()` constructor instead of using
{meth}`api.world.World.get_block_instance` is supported but not encouraged. To avoid possible caveats of doing this,
make sure to either only instantiate blocks with Amulet blockstate data or use
{meth}`api.world.World.get_block_instance` instead
:::

Here's a few examples on how create a Block object with extra blocks:

Creating a new Block object with the base of `stone` and has an extra block of `water[level=1]`:

```pycon
>>> stone = Block.from_string_blockstate("minecraft:stone")
>>> water_level_1 = Block.from_string_blockstate("minecraft:water[level=1]")
>>> stone_with_extra_block = stone + water_level_1
>>> repr(stone_with_extra_block)
'Block(minecraft:stone, minecraft:water[level=1])'
```

Creating a new Block object using the namespace and base_name:

```pycon
>>> granite = Block(namespace="minecraft", base_name="granite")
```

Creating a new Block object with another layer of extra blocks:

```pycon
>>> stone_water_granite = stone_with_extra_block + granite # Doesn't modify any of the other objects
>>> repr(stone_water_granite)
'Block(minecraft:stone, minecraft:water[level=1], minecraft:granite)'
```

Creating a new Block object by removing an extra block from all layers:

*Note: This removes all instances of the Block object from extra blocks*

```pycon
>>> stone_granite = stone_water_granite - water_level_1 # Doesn't modify any of the other objects either
>>> repr(stone_granite)
'Block(minecraft:stone, minecraft:granite)'
```

Creating a new Block object by removing a specific layer:

```pycon
>>> oak_log_axis_x = Block.from_string_blockstate("minecraft:oak_log[axis=x]")
>>> stone_water_granite_water_oak_log = stone_water_granite + water_level_1 + oak_log_axis_x
>>> repr(stone_water_granite_water_oak_log)
'Block(minecraft:stone, minecraft:water[level=1], minecraft:granite, minecraft:water[level=1], minecraft:oak_log[axis=x])'
```

```pycon
>>> stone_granite_water_oak_log = stone_water_granite_water_oak_log.remove_layer(0)
>>> repr(stone_granite_water_oak_log)
'Block(minecraft:stone, minecraft:granite, minecraft:water[level=1], minecraft:oak_log[axis=x])'
```
